subject: ap-chemistry
title: Potassium Sorbate Titration & Buffer
stimulus:
Potassium sorbate, KC$_6$H$_7$O$_2$ (molar mass 150. g/mol) is commonly added to diet soft drinks as a preservative.
A stock solution of KC$_6$H$_7$O$_2$(aq) of known concentration must be prepared. A student titrates 45.00 mL of the
stock solution with 1.25 M HCl(aq) using both an indicator and a pH meter. The value of $K_a$ for sorbic acid,
HC$_6$H$_7$O$_2$, is 1.7 Г— 10$^{-5}$.

parts.prompt: Write the net-ionic equation for the reaction between KC$_6$H$_7$O$_2$(aq) and HCl(aq).
parts.scoring_notes: The sorbate anion (C$_6$H$_7$O$_2^-$) is a weak base that reacts with the strong acid HCl. Net ionic equation: C$_6$H$_7$O$_2^-$(aq) + H$^+$(aq) вҶ’ HC$_6$H$_7$O$_2$(aq). K$^+$ and Cl$^-$ are spectator ions.

scoring_points.description: For the correct balanced net-ionic equation: 1 point
wrong_examples: Including K$^+$ or Cl$^-$ вҖ” these are spectator ions and must be omitted in the net-ionic equation.
Writing the molecular equation instead of net-ionic.
common_traps: The net-ionic equation omits spectator ions: K$^+$ (from KC$_6$H$_7$O$_2$) and Cl$^-$ (from HCl) are spectators.

parts.prompt:
A total of 29.95 mL of 1.25 M HCl(aq) is required to reach the equivalence point. Calculate [KC$_6$H$_7$O$_2$]
in the stock solution.
parts.scoring_notes: At equivalence: moles HCl = moles sorbate. $n$(HCl) = (1.25 M)(0.02995 L) = 0.03744 mol. [KC$_6$H$_7$O$_2$] = 0.03744 mol / 0.04500 L = 0.832 M.

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated concentration with proper stoichiometry: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
$n(\text{HCl}) = (1.25)(0.02995) = 0.03744\text{ mol}$
At equivalence: $n$(sorbate) = $n$(HCl) = 0.03744 mol (1:1 stoichiometry)
$[\text{KC}_6\text{H}_7\text{O}_2] = 0.03744 / 0.04500 = 0.832\text{ M}$

wrong_examples: Using 29.95 mL as the volume of sorbate solution instead of HCl.
common_traps: Divide by the volume of the sorbate stock solution (45.00 mL = 0.04500 L), not the HCl volume.

parts.prompt:
The pH at the equivalence point of the titration is measured to be 2.54. Which of the following indicators
would be the best choice for determining the end point of the titration? Justify your answer.
pK$_a$
Indicator:
Phenolphthalein 9.3 | Bromothymol blue 7.0 | Methyl red 5.0 | Thymol blue 2.0 | Methyl violet 0.80
parts.scoring_notes: Thymol blue (pK$_a$ = 2.0) is the best choice. The indicator should change color near the equivalence point pH of 2.54. The ideal indicator has a pK$_a$ within approximately 1 unit of the equivalence point pH. Thymol blue (pK$_a$ = 2.0) gives a color change in the range ~1вҖ“3, which brackets 2.54.

scoring_points.description: For thymol blue with reasoning that its pK$_a$ is closest to equivalence point pH: 1 point

required_elements: Its pK$_a$ = 2.0 is closest to the equivalence point pH of 2.54; it changes color in the range that includes pH 2.54.
alternatives.correct_example: Thymol blue is the best choice. An indicator's color change occurs near its pK$_a$ (in the range pK$_a$ Вұ 1). Thymol blue has pK$_a$ = 2.0, so its color change occurs between ~pH 1 and pH 3, which includes the equivalence point pH of 2.54. The other indicators change color at much higher pH values and would not signal the correct endpoint.

wrong_examples: Methyl violet вҖ” its pK$_a$ = 0.80 is too far below the equivalence point pH of 2.54.
Methyl red вҖ” it changes color around pH 4вҖ“6, far from the 2.54 equivalence point.
common_traps: Choose the indicator whose pK$_a$ is closest to the equivalence point pH. Color change occurs in the range pK$_a$ Вұ 1.

parts.prompt: Calculate the pH at the half-equivalence point.
parts.scoring_notes: At the half-equivalence point, [HC$_6$H$_7$O$_2$] = [C$_6$H$_7$O$_2^-$]. By HendersonвҖ“Hasselbalch: pH = pK$_a$ = вҲ’log(1.7 Г— 10$^{-5}$) = 4.77.

scoring_points.description: For pH = pK$_a$ = 4.77 at the half-equivalence point: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
At the half-equivalence point, half the sorbate has been protonated: [HC$_6$H$_7$O$_2$] = [C$_6$H$_7$O$_2^-$]. By HendersonвҖ“Hasselbalch: pH = pK$_a$ + log([A$^-$]/[HA]) = pK$_a$ + log(1) = pK$_a$.
pK$_a$ = $-\log(1.7\times10^{-5}) = 4.77$.

wrong_examples: Using the equivalence point pH of 2.54 вҖ” the half-equivalence and equivalence points are different.
Calculating from ICE table instead of using HendersonвҖ“Hasselbalch shortcut.
common_traps: At the half-equivalence point: [HA] = [A$^-$], so pH = pK$_a$. This is a shortcut that avoids an ICE table.

parts.prompt:
The initial pH and the equivalence point are plotted on the graph below. Accurately sketch the titration curve
on the graph below. Mark the position of the half-equivalence point on the curve with an X.
parts.scoring_notes: The titration curve starts at an acidic-to-neutral-looking pH (basic salt solution titrated with acid), decreases through a gradual region at the half-equivalence point (X at ~half the equivalence volume), drops steeply at the equivalence point, then levels off at low pH. Must place X at half the equivalence volume at pH вүҲ 4.77.

parts.prompt:
The pH of the soft drink is 3.37 after the addition of the KC$_6$H$_7$O$_2$(aq). Which species, HC$_6$H$_7$O$_2$
or C$_6$H$_7$O$_2^-$, has a higher concentration in the soft drink? Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: pH = 3.37, pK$_a$ = 4.77. Since pH < pK$_a$, the acid form (HC$_6$H$_7$O$_2$) predominates. By HendersonвҖ“Hasselbalch: 3.37 = 4.77 + log([A$^-$]/[HA]), so log([A$^-$]/[HA]) = вҲ’1.40, meaning [HA] >> [A$^-$].

scoring_points.description: For HC$_6$H$_7$O$_2$ with correct HendersonвҖ“Hasselbalch or pK$_a$ reasoning: 1 point

required_elements: HC$_6$H$_7$O$_2$ (sorbic acid) has the higher concentration.
pH (3.37) < pK$_a$ (4.77), so the acid form predominates over the conjugate base.
alternatives.correct_example: HC$_6$H$_7$O$_2$ has the higher concentration. The pH (3.37) is less than the pK$_a$ (4.77). When pH < pK$_a$, the acid form [HA] is greater than the conjugate base [A$^-$]. Using HendersonвҖ“Hasselbalch: $\log([\text{A}^-]/[\text{HA}]) = 3.37 - 4.77 = -1.40$, meaning $[\text{HA}]/[\text{A}^-] = 10^{1.40} \approx 25$.

wrong_examples: C$_6$H$_7$O$_2^-$ вҖ” when pH < pK$_a$, the solution is more acidic than the half-equivalence point, so the acid form dominates.
common_traps: pH < pK$_a$ вҶ’ acid form [HA] dominates. pH > pK$_a$ вҶ’ conjugate base [A$^-$] dominates. pH = pK$_a$ вҶ’ [HA] = [A$^-$].
